subject: History
chapter: The Making of a Global World

activities_projects.title: Mapping the Silk Routes
activities_projects.objective: Identify the vast network of pre-modern global connectivity
steps: Trace the land routes connecting China to the Mediterranean and sea routes via the Indian Ocean
Mark key commodities exchanged: Chinese silk, Indian spices, and Precious metals (gold/silver) from Europe
Locate regions where Buddhism and Christianity spread through these routes
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Spatial understanding of cultural and economic exchange before the 15th century
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of routes and commodity placement (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Columbian Exchange Menu
activities_projects.objective: Deconstruct the global journey of everyday food items
steps: Create a 'Before 1492' and 'After 1492' menu for an Indian or European kitchen
Identify foods that came from the Americas (Potatoes, Maize, Tomatoes, Chilies)
Explain how the introduction of the potato led to the 'Irish Potato Famine' later in history
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Grasps the concept of biological and agricultural globalization
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of food origins and historical links (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Rinderpest: The Cattle Plague Storyboard
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the impact of biological disease on colonial expansion
steps: Draw a 4-panel storyboard illustrating: 1. Arrival of infected cattle in Africa, 2. The death of 90% of local livestock, 3. The destruction of African livelihoods, 4. Locals forced into the colonial labor market
Write a short caption for each panel explaining the socio-economic impact
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands how disease facilitated European conquest of Africa
activities_projects.evaluation: Historical narrative clarity and accuracy (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Indentured Labour: A New Slavery Audit
activities_projects.objective: Examine the conditions and cultural legacy of Indian migrants
steps: Locate the main regions of departure (UP, Bihar, Central India) and arrival (Fiji, Trinidad, Mauritius) on a map
Research the living conditions on plantations and the 'false promises' made by recruiters
Briefly describe the cultural synthesis that followed (e.g., Chutney music or Hosay festival)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analysis of the human cost of global economic integration

activities_projects.title: The Great Depression: Economic Domino Effect
activities_projects.objective: Map the global repercussions of the 1929 US stock market crash
steps: Create a flowchart: US bank failures -> Withdrawal of international loans -> Collapse of European currencies -> Fall in global commodity prices
Plot a simple graph showing the decline of Indian wheat or jute exports between 1928 and 1934
Analyze why agricultural regions suffered more than industrial regions
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understanding global economic interdependency and volatility
activities_projects.evaluation: Logical flow of causality and data accuracy (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Henry Ford's Assembly Line Simulation
activities_projects.objective: Evaluate the benefits and stresses of mass production
steps: Assign students specific repetitive tasks to 'assemble' a product on a desk 'conveyor belt'
Gradually increase the speed and record the 'output' vs 'worker stress' levels
Discuss why Ford had to double the daily wage to five dollars to keep his workers
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Grasps the concept of 'Fordism' and industrial labor dynamics
activities_projects.evaluation: Reflective analysis of the simulation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Bretton Woods Institutions Fact-File
activities_projects.objective: Examine the post-WWII global financial architecture
steps: Define the roles of the IMF (International Monetary Fund) and the IBRD (World Bank)
Explain the 'fixed exchange rate' system based on the US Dollar and Gold
Describe why the G-77 group was formed by developing nations later
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Knowledge of the institutions governing the modern global economy
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of institutional functions and economic logic (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Triangular Trade Cycle
activities_projects.objective: Visualize the brutal logic of 18th-century Atlantic trade
materials: Outline map of the Atlantic Ocean
steps: Draw the triangle connecting Europe, Africa, and the Americas
Label what was moved on each leg: 1. Manufactured goods to Africa, 2. Slaves to Americas, 3. Raw materials (sugar/cotton) to Europe
Explain how this trade fueled the Industrial Revolution in Britain
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understanding the link between slavery, colonialism, and industrial growth
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct mapping and labeling of the cycle (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Opium Trade and China: A Case Study
activities_projects.objective: Trace the forced commercialization of trade in the 19th century
steps: Map the flow: Britain (Finance) -> India (Opium production) -> China (Consumption/Export of Tea/Silk)
Explain how opium profits helped the British pay for Chinese tea imports
Write a short note on the 'Drain of Wealth' from India during this period
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Insights into how colonial trade balanced imperial deficits
activities_projects.evaluation: Analysis of trade flow and economic consequences (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Technological Nexus: Refrigerated Ships
activities_projects.objective: Assess how technology altered global diet and social peace
steps: Compare meat transport before and after refrigerated ships (live cattle vs frozen meat)
Explain how lower meat prices in Europe reduced social unrest among the poor
Identify the major meat exporting regions (America, Australia, New Zealand)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the intersection of technology, diet, and political stability
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of technological impact and geographic links (10 marks)
